   454→
   455→## Chapter 2: Railway Domain Fundamentals
   456→
   457→### 2.1 Essential Glossary
   458→
   459→All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.
   460→
   461→**Core Terms:**
   462→
   463→| Term | Definition | NOT Acceptable |
   464→|------|------------|----------------|
   465→| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
   466→| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
   467→| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
   468→| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
   469→| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
   470→| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
   471→| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
   472→| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |
   473→
   474→**Maintenance Terms:**
   475→
   476→| Term | Definition | Typical Interval |
   477→|------|------------|------------------|
   478→| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
   479→| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
   480→| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
   481→| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |
   482→
   483→**Operational Terms:**
   484→
   485→| Term | Definition |
   486→|------|------------|
   487→| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
   488→| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
   489→| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
   490→| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
   491→| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |
   492→
   493→### 2.2 Business Entities & Relationships
   494→
   495→**Entity Relationship Model:**
   496→
   497→```
   498→┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐
   499→│    Fleet    │────────│    Train    │
   500→│             │   1:N  │             │
   501→│ - name      │        │ - train_id  │
   502→│ - pm1_km    │        │ - current_km│
   503→│ - pm2_km    │        │ - target_km │
   504→│ - hm_km     │        │ - fitness   │
   505→└─────────────┘        └──────┬──────┘
   506→                              │
   507→       ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
   508→       │                      │                      │
   509→       ▼                      ▼                      ▼
   510→┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐
   511→│    Depot    │        │ Allocation  │        │ Maintenance │
   512→│             │        │             │        │  Schedule   │
   513→│ - name      │        │ - date      │        │             │
   514→│ - capacity  │        │ - status    │        │ - type      │
   515→│ - manhours  │        │ - score     │        │ - date      │
   516→└─────────────┘        │ - reasons   │        │ - depot     │
   517→                       └──────┬──────┘        └─────────────┘
   518→                              │
   519→                              ▼
   520→                       ┌─────────────┐
   521→                       │   Service   │
   522→                       │             │
   523→                       │ - code      │
   524→                       │ - origin    │
   525→                       │ - dest      │
   526→                       │ - distance  │
   527→                       └─────────────┘
   528→```
   529→
   530→**Cardinality Rules:**
   531→
   532→- One Fleet has many Trains (1:N)
   533→- One Train belongs to one Fleet (N:1)
   534→- One Depot can host trains from multiple Fleets (N:M)
   535→- One Train has one Base Depot (N:1)
   536→- One Service can have many Allocations (different dates) (1:N)
   537→- One Train can have many Allocations (different services) (1:N)
   538→- One Train can have many Maintenance Schedules (1:N)
   539→
   540→### 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy
   541→
   542→**Maintenance Cascade:**
   543→
   544→```
   545→Fleet Type (e.g., Metro Series 3400)
   546→ └─ Maintenance Profile
   547→     ├─ PM1 (every 15,000 km)
   548→     │   └─ Duration: 1 day
   549→     │   └─ Tasks: Inspection, fluid top-up, brake check
   550→     │
   551→     ├─ PM2 (every 45,000 km)
   552→     │   └─ Duration: 3 days
   553→     │   └─ Tasks: Component replacement, deep inspection
   554→     │   └─ Note: Includes PM1 tasks
   555→     │
   556→     ├─ HM (every 90,000 km)
   557→     │   └─ Duration: 7-14 days
   558→     │   └─ Tasks: Major component overhaul, bogie work
   559→     │   └─ Note: Includes PM1 + PM2 tasks
   560→     │
   561→     └─ OVH (every 6 years or 500,000 km)
   562→         └─ Duration: 30-60 days
   563→         └─ Tasks: Complete refurbishment
   564→         └─ Note: Resets maintenance counters
   565→```
   566→
   567→**Train State Machine:**
   568→
   569→```
   570→    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
   571→    │                                             │
   572→    ▼                                             │
   573→┌───────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐  │
   574→│  FIT  │────▶│ IN-SERVICE │────▶│ AVAILABLE  │──┘
   575→│       │     │            │     │            │
   576→└───┬───┘     └────────────┘     └─────┬──────┘
   577→    │                                  │
   578→    │         ┌────────────┐           │
   579→    └────────▶│   UNFIT    │◀──────────┘
   580→              │            │
   581→              └─────┬──────┘
   582→                    │
   583→                    ▼
   584→              ┌────────────┐
   585→              │MAINTENANCE │
   586→              │            │
   587→              └─────┬──────┘
   588→                    │
   589→                    ▼
   590→              ┌────────────┐
   591→              │    FIT     │
   592→              └────────────┘
   593→```
   594→
   595→### 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)
   596→
   597→These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.
   598→
   599→**Hard Constraints (Never Violate):**
   600→
   601→1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
   602→2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
   603→3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
   604→4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
   605→5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization
   606→
   607→**Soft Constraints (Optimize Within):**
   608→
   609→1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
   610→2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
   611→3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
   612→4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)
   613→
   614→**Critical Timing Rules:**
   615→
   616→| Rule | Specification |
   617→|------|---------------|
   618→| PM1 tolerance window | Can be ±500 km from trigger |
   619→| PM2 tolerance window | Can be ±1,000 km from trigger |
   620→| HM tolerance window | Can be ±2,000 km from trigger |
   621→| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
   622→| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |
   623→
   624→### 2.5 ECM Certification Framework (EU Regulation 2019/779)
   625→
   626→MMS aligns with the Entity in Charge of Maintenance (ECM) certification requirements as defined by EU Regulation 2019/779. This framework establishes the legal responsibilities for railway vehicle maintenance across the European railway network.
   627→
   628→**ECM Function Mapping:**
   629→
   630→| Function | Responsibility | MMS Feature |
   631→|----------|----------------|-------------|
   632→| **ECM-F1** | Maintenance Management | Dashboard KPIs, compliance tracking, audit trail |
   633→| **ECM-F2** | Maintenance Development | Scheduler optimization, plan generation, interval definitions |
   634→| **ECM-F3** | Fleet Maintenance Management | Real-time fleet status, conflict detection, availability monitoring |
   635→| **ECM-F4** | Maintenance Delivery | Work order generation, depot allocation, resource planning |
   636→
   637→**Compliance Mapping:**
   638→
   639→| Requirement | ECM Article | MMS Implementation |
   640→|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
   641→| Risk assessment | Art. 5.2 | Conflict detection, overdue warnings, overrun alerts |
   642→| Competence management | Art. 5.3 | Future: resource skill tracking (Phase 2+) |
   643→| Documentation | Art. 5.4 | Audit trail, maintenance history, decision logging |
   644→| Performance monitoring | Art. 5.5 | KPI dashboard, fleet availability metrics, variance tracking |
   645→
   646→**Critical Note:** The ECM framework requires that the entity responsible for maintenance (operator or subcontractor) maintains full traceability of all maintenance decisions. MMS provides this through:
   647→- Immutable audit logs of all scheduling decisions
   648→- Justification records for allocation recommendations
   649→- Historical mileage tracking with timestamps
   650→
   651→### 2.6 RAMS Compliance (EN 50126-1)
   652→
   653→MMS supports RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Safety) principles as defined in EN 50126-1, the European standard for railway dependability.
   654→
   655→**RAMS Integration:**
   656→
   657→| Aspect | Standard | MMS Feature | Target |
   658→|--------|----------|-------------|--------|
   659→| **Reliability** | EN 50126-1 §6 | MTBF tracking, failure prediction | TBD by operator |
   660→| **Availability** | EN 50126-1 §7 | Fleet availability KPIs, downtime monitoring | 95-99% |
   661→| **Maintainability** | EN 50126-1 §8 | MTTR optimization, workload balancing | <24h per PM1 |
   662→| **Safety** | EN 50126-1 §9 | Critical task prioritization, overrun prevention | Zero safety overruns |
   663→
   664→**Key Performance Indicators:**
   665→
   666→| KPI | Formula | Target | Critical Threshold |
   667→|-----|---------|--------|-------------------|
   668→| Fleet Availability | (Fit Trains / Total Trains) × 100 | ≥97% | <95% triggers alert |
   669→| MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | Total Operating Hours / Number of Failures | TBD | Operator-defined |
   670→| MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | Total Downtime / Number of Repairs | ≤24h | >48h triggers escalation |
   671→| Schedule Adherence | (On-Time Maintenance / Scheduled Maintenance) × 100 | ≥95% | <90% requires review |
   672→
   673→### 2.7 Terminology Equivalence
   674→
   675→MMS uses aviation-derived terminology (A/B/C/D Checks) internally for developer familiarity, but provides full equivalence mapping to railway standards for operator communication.
   676→
   677→**Maintenance Level Equivalence:**
   678→
   679→| MMS Term | Railway Standard (EN 15380) | Aviation Origin | Typical Interval | Duration |
   680→|----------|----------------------------|-----------------|--------------------|----------|
   681→| A-Check | PM1 (Daily/Service) | Line maintenance | Daily / 15,000 km | 15-60 min |
   682→| B-Check | PM2 (Intermediate) | A-Check | 45,000-50,000 km | 4-16 hours |
   683→| C-Check | PM3 (Scheduled) | C-Check | 90,000-100,000 km | 1-3 days |
   684→| D-Overhaul | HM (Heavy Maintenance) | D-Check | 500,000-800,000 km | 2-6 weeks |
   685→
   686→> **Note:** MMS uses aviation-derived terminology (A/B/C/D) internally for developer familiarity. All user-facing displays and reports use EN 15380 railway terminology (PM1/PM2/PM3/HM) to maintain credibility with railway professionals.
   687→
   688→**UIC IRS Reference:**
   689→
   690→The International Union of Railways (UIC) publishes International Railway Solutions (IRS) that complement EN standards:
   691→
   692→| Document | Title | Relevance to MMS |
   693→|----------|-------|------------------|
   694→| IRS 30100-3 | Maintenance Terms | Terminology standardization |
   695→| IRS 30200 | RAM Demonstrable values | KPI calculation methods |
   696→| IRS 70711 | Rolling Stock Maintenance | Maintenance interval guidelines |
   697→
   698→### 2.8 What the System Does NOT Do
   699→
   700→Explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and set correct expectations:
   701→
   702→**Phase 2+ (Not in Demo):**
   703→
   704→| Feature | Reason for Exclusion | Target Phase |
   705→|---------|---------------------|--------------|
   706→| MILP long-term optimization | Exceeds complexity budget | Phase 2 |
   707→| Real EAM system integration | Requires client environment | Phase 2 |
   708→| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
   709→| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
   710→| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
   711→| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
   712→| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
   713→| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |
   714→
   715→**Explicitly NOT Goals:**
   716→
   717→- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
   718→- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
   719→- This is NOT a timetabling system
   720→- This is NOT a revenue management system
   721→- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
   722→- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems
   723→
   724→---
   725→
   726→## Chapter 3: Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning
   727→
   728→### 3.1 Competitor Landscape
   729→
   730→**Primary Competitors:**
   731→
   732→| Competitor | Focus Area | Strength | Weakness |
   733→|------------|------------|----------|----------|
   734→| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
   735→| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
   736→| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
   737→| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |
   738→
   739→### 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis
   740→
   741→SignatureRail represents the most direct competitor in terms of user experience and feature set.
   742→
   743→**SignatureRail Strengths:**
   744→
   745→- Clean, modern enterprise UI
   746→- Well-organized workflow panels
   747→- Good use of data tables
   748→- Professional color scheme
   749→- Clear navigation structure
   750→
   751→**SignatureRail Weaknesses (MMS Opportunities):**
   752→
   753→1. **Basic km tracking without optimization** - Shows variance but doesn't recommend actions
   754→2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
   755→3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios
   756→4. **Separate maintenance and operations** - Silos remain
   757→5. **No map visualization** - Spatial context missing
   758→
   759→### 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities
   760→
   761→**Gap Analysis:**
   762→
   763→| Gap | Market Need | MMS Solution |
   764→|-----|-------------|--------------|
   765→| Passive vs Active Balancing | Systems show variance but don't optimize | AI-powered recommendations with justifications |
   766→| Black Box Decisions | Users don't understand why system recommends | Transparent 3+ reason justifications |
   767→| Operations/Maintenance Silos | Separate systems, manual coordination | Unified interface for both departments |
   768→| Strategic Planning | Day-to-day focus, no simulation | What-if scenarios for planning |
   769→| Dead Running Visibility | Hidden cost, rarely tracked | Explicit display and optimization |
   770→
   771→### 3.4 MMS Differentiators
   772→
   773→**Primary Differentiators:**
   774→
   775→1. **Km Balancing as Core Objective**
   776→   - Not a secondary feature or afterthought
   777→   - Drives all allocation recommendations
   778→   - Visible in dashboard KPIs
   779→
   780→2. **Transparent Justifications**
   781→   - Every recommendation shows 3+ reasons
   782→   - Human-readable explanations
   783→   - Builds trust vs black box
   784→
   785→3. **Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations**
   786→   - Single interface for both departments
   787→   - Recommendations consider maintenance schedule
   788→   - Reduces coordination overhead
   789→
   790→4. **Accessible Simulation**
   791→   - Decision-makers can test scenarios
   792→   - Side-by-side comparison
   793→   - No technical expertise required
   794→
   795→5. **Visible Dead Running**
   796→   - Hidden cost made explicit
   797→   - Factored into optimization
   798→   - ROI clearly demonstrable
   799→
   800→---
   801→
   802→## Chapter 4: Demo Strategy & Presentation
   803→
   804→### 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth
   805→
   806→> **Mantra:** "Solving 1 pain point extremely well > solving 10 pain points poorly"
   807→
   808→**Strategic Focus:**
   809→
   810→The demo should demonstrate deep understanding of the km balancing problem rather than showcase many features superficially. Every feature shown must:
   811→
   812→1. Be 100% functional (no "coming soon")
   813→2. Use correct railway terminology
   814→3. Show real (simulated but realistic) data
   815→4. Complete end-to-end without errors
   816→
   817→### 4.2 Wow Moments
   818→
   819→These are specific moments in the demo designed to create impact:
   820→
   821→**Wow Moment 1: The Variance Discovery**
   822→- Dashboard loads showing 18% variance (yellow warning)
   823→- Client recognizes their exact problem
   824→- "They're showing us what we live every day"
   825→
   826→**Wow Moment 2: The Justification Card**
   827→- Recommendation shows not just WHAT but WHY
   828→- 3+ human-readable reasons
   829→- "Finally, a system that explains itself"
   830→
   831→**Wow Moment 3: The What-If**
   832→- Create scenario, adjust allocations
   833→- See projected impact in < 10 seconds
   834→- "I can test my ideas before committing"
   835→
   836→**Wow Moment 4: The Map Context**
   837→- See where all trains are spatially
   838→- Click train, see km delta immediately
   839→- "This gives us operational awareness"
   840→
   841→### 4.3 Credibility Checklist
   842→
   843→Before demo, verify:
   844→
   845→- [ ] All terminology is EN 15380 compliant
   846→- [ ] Depot names are real Portuguese locations
   847→- [ ] Service codes follow realistic patterns (IC, R, U)
   848→- [ ] Maintenance intervals are realistic (PM1: 15k km)
   849→- [ ] Train IDs follow realistic naming (UTE-3400-xxx)
   850→- [ ] Km values are in plausible ranges (30k-60k)
   851→- [ ] Variance percentages are realistic (10-25%)
   852→- [ ] Map shows Portugal correctly positioned
   853→
   854→### 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid
   855→
   856→**NEVER do these in demo:**
   857→
   858→| Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Alternative |
   859→|--------------|--------------|-------------|
   860→| Show stub screens | Destroys credibility | Show fewer, complete features |
   861→| Use placeholder data | "Lorem ipsum" is unprofessional | Use realistic railway data |
   862→| Demo features that don't work | Client will try to break it | Only show stable features |
   863→| Use generic software terms | "Vehicle" instead of "Train" | Use railway terminology |
   864→| Show loading spinners | Suggests slow system | Pre-cache or mock fast responses |
   865→| Apologize for missing features | Shows lack of confidence | Present what exists as intentional |
   866→
   867→### 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)
   868→
   869→**Minutes 0-2: Context Setting**
   870→- "We understand your km balancing challenge"
   871→- Show dashboard with variance indicator
   872→- "This is what you see every day"
   873→
   874→**Minutes 2-5: The Problem Quantified**
   875→- Click variance to see distribution
   876→- Show outliers (5 over-running, 4 under-running)
   877→- "These 9 trains are costing you money"
   878→
   879→**Minutes 5-8: The Solution in Action**
   880→- Navigate to Allocation
   881→- Select a pending service
   882→- Show recommendations with justifications
   883→- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
   884→- Confirm allocation with one click
   885→
   886→**Minutes 8-11: Planning Ahead**
   887→- Navigate to Simulation
   888→- Create what-if scenario
   889→- Adjust allocations for under-running trains
   890→- Show projected variance improvement
   891→- "Test your strategy before committing"
   892→
   893→**Minutes 11-13: The Big Picture**
   894→- Return to Dashboard
   895→- Show map with train positions
   896→- Show depot capacity
   897→- "Everything in one view"
   898→
   899→**Minutes 13-15: Q&A Buffer**
   900→- Prepared questions if no questions asked
   901→- Have fallback screens ready
   902→
   903→### 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans
   904→
   905→**Anticipated Questions:**
   906→
   907→| Question | Prepared Answer |
   908→|----------|-----------------|
   909→| "How does the solver work?" | "CP-SAT optimization balancing multiple constraints - we can show the logic" |
   910→| "Can it integrate with our EAM?" | "Yes, REST API design allows integration with any EAM system" |
   911→| "What about real-time GPS?" | "Architecture supports real-time feeds, demo uses simulated positions" |
   912→| "How long to implement?" | "Core system in 3 months, customization depends on requirements" |
   913→| "What if solver times out?" | "Fallback to simpler ranking ensures always having recommendations" |
   914→
   915→**Fallback Plans:**
   916→
   917→| Failure | Fallback |
   918→|---------|----------|
   919→| Solver timeout | Pre-computed recommendations in database |
   920→| Database connection lost | Static JSON fallback data |
   921→| Map fails to load | Table view of train positions |
   922→| Browser crash | Second laptop with same setup |
   923→| Network issues | Everything runs locally |
   924→
   925→---
   926→
   927→# PART II: USER EXPERIENCE & DESIGN
   928→
   929→---
   930→
   931→## Chapter 5: User Personas
   932→
   933→### 5.1 Paul - The Maintenance Planner
   934→
   935→**Profile:**
   936→
   937→| Attribute | Value |
   938→|-----------|-------|
   939→| Name | Paul |
   940→| Age | 45 years |
   941→| Role | Maintenance Planner |
   942→| Experience | 18 years in railway maintenance |
   943→| Education | Mechanical Engineering degree |
   944→
   945→**Work Context:**
   946→
   947→| Attribute | Value |
   948→|-----------|-------|
   949→| Daily System Usage | 4-8 hours |
   950→| Primary Device | Desktop with large monitor (27"+) |
   951→| Secondary Device | Tablet for depot floor checks |
   952→| Work Hours | 07:00 - 16:00 (day shift) |
   953→| Team Size | Reports to Maintenance Manager, works with 3 other planners |
   954→
   955→**Goals:**
   956→
   957→1. Schedule preventive maintenance minimizing operational impact
   958→2. Balance depot capacity across weeks
   959→3. Ensure no train exceeds maintenance tolerance windows
   960→4. Coordinate with Operations to minimize service disruption
   961→5. Reduce dead running for maintenance movements
   962→
   963→**Frustrations:**
   964→
   965→1. "I spend hours in Excel trying to balance the schedule"
   966→2. "Operations changes plans without telling me"
   967→3. "I can't see impact of changes until it's too late"
   968→4. "Every depot is managed separately, no fleet-wide view"
   969→5. "Constant firefighting because maintenance gets pushed"
   970→
   971→**Success Moment:**
   972→> "I can see the whole fleet's km distribution and immediately know which trains need attention."
   973→
   974→**Critical Need:** Gantt chart with drag-drop functionality showing depot capacity
   975→
   976→### 5.2 Diana - The Dispatcher
   977→
   978→**Profile:**
   979→
   980→| Attribute | Value |
   981→|-----------|-------|
   982→| Name | Diana |
   983→| Age | 38 years |
   984→| Role | Dispatcher (Operations Controller) |
   985→| Experience | 12 years in railway operations |
   986→| Education | Transportation Management diploma |
   987→
   988→**Work Context:**
   989→
   990→| Attribute | Value |
   991→|-----------|-------|
   992→| Daily System Usage | Continuous during 8-hour shift |
   993→| Primary Device | Desktop with multiple monitors (2-3) |
   994→| Secondary Device | Radio/phone for depot communication |
   995→| Work Hours | Rotating shifts (06:00-14:00, 14:00-22:00, 22:00-06:00) |
   996→| Team Size | 2-3 dispatchers per shift |
   997→
   998→**Goals:**
   999→
  1000→1. Allocate trains to services as quickly as possible

# MMS - Metro Mileage Management System
## Complete Product & Technical Specification

**Version:** 2.0.0
**Date:** 2026-01-05
**Status:** Definitive Specification
**Audience:** UX/Product Team, Development Team, Stakeholders

---

## Document Purpose

This document serves as the **Single Source of Truth** for MMS (Metro Mileage Management System).

---

## Table of Contents

### Part I: Business Context & Strategy

#### 1. Executive Summary
- 1.1 Project Overview
- 1.2 Business Objective
- 1.3 Success Metrics
- 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

> **Covers:** High-level project goals, the competitive bid context, what success looks like, and the 12-week timeline with key milestones.

#### 2. Railway Domain Fundamentals
- 2.1 Essential Glossary
- 2.2 Business Entities & Relationships
- 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy
- 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)
- 2.5 What the System Does NOT Do

> **Covers:** Railway-specific terminology (EN 15380), entity relationships, maintenance intervals (PM1/PM2/HM), and domain constraints that are critical for credibility.

#### 3. Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning
- 3.1 Competitor Landscape
- 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis
- 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities
- 3.4 MMS Differentiators

> **Covers:** Analysis of DELMIA Quintiq, Tracsis, Railnova, SignatureRail; identified gaps in the market; and how MMS positions itself.

#### 4. Demo Strategy & Presentation
- 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth
- 4.2 Wow Moments
- 4.3 Credibility Checklist
- 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid
- 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)
- 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans

> **Covers:** How to win the bid through impressive demo, key moments that create impact, presentation flow, and contingency plans.

---

### Part II: User Experience & Design

#### 5. User Personas
- 5.1 Paul - The Maintenance Planner
- 5.2 Diana - The Dispatcher
- 5.3 Gabriel - The Fleet Manager
- 5.4 Design Implications per Persona

> **Covers:** Detailed persona profiles including goals, frustrations, device preferences, and how each persona influences UI decisions.

#### 6. User Journeys
- 6.1 Journey 1: Allocating a Train to Service (Diana)
- 6.2 Journey 2: Identifying and Resolving Imbalance (Paul)
- 6.3 Journey 3: Planning Maintenance Schedule (Paul)
- 6.4 Journey 4: Executive Review (Gabriel)
- 6.5 Journey Requirements Matrix

> **Covers:** Step-by-step user flows with scenes, expected times, click counts, and requirements revealed by each journey.

#### 7. Information Architecture
- 7.1 Navigation Structure
- 7.2 Drill-Down Hierarchy
- 7.3 Screen Inventory
- 7.4 Component Relationships

> **Covers:** How screens connect, navigation patterns, progressive disclosure hierarchy, and mental model alignment.

#### 8. Visual Design System
- 8.1 Design Principles
- 8.2 Color Palette & Semantic Colors
- 8.3 Typography Scale
- 8.4 Spacing & Layout Grid
- 8.5 Component Library (Blueprint.js v6)
- 8.6 Dark Mode Specification
- 8.7 Railway-Specific Visual Patterns

> **Covers:** Complete design token system, Blueprint.js integration, dark mode requirements, and enterprise UI patterns.

#### 9. Screen Specifications
- 9.1 Dashboard (Home)
- 9.2 Map View
- 9.3 Allocation Screen
- 9.4 Balancing Analysis
- 9.5 Simulation / What-If
- 9.6 Maintenance Gantt
- 9.7 Fleet List
- 9.8 Train Details

> **Covers:** Wireframes, component placement, data requirements, interactions, and acceptance criteria for each screen.

---

### Part III: Functional Requirements

#### 10. Requirements Overview
- 10.1 Functional Requirements Matrix
- 10.2 Non-Functional Requirements
- 10.3 Priority Classification (P0/P1/P2)
- 10.4 Scope Boundaries

> **Covers:** Complete FR/NFR tables with priorities, what's in scope vs explicitly out of scope.

#### 11. Module: Dashboard & KPIs
- 11.1 KPI Cards Specification
- 11.2 Traffic-Light Indicators
- 11.3 Depot Capacity Visualization
- 11.4 Maintenance Alerts
- 11.5 Drill-Down Navigation

> **Covers:** Detailed specs for the dashboard module with acceptance criteria and edge cases.

#### 12. Module: Map & Fleet Visualization
- 12.1 Map Provider & Configuration
- 12.2 Train Markers & Status Colors
- 12.3 Depot Markers & Capacity Indicators
- 12.4 Train Selection & Popup Cards
- 12.5 Auto-Refresh Mechanism
- 12.6 Clustering Behavior

> **Covers:** Map implementation details, marker specifications, and real-time update requirements.

#### 13. Module: Allocation with Recommendations
- 13.1 Service List & Filtering
- 13.2 Recommendation Engine Integration
- 13.3 Justification Display
- 13.4 One-Click Allocation
- 13.5 Train Filtering Criteria

> **Covers:** The core allocation workflow, solver integration, and justification presentation.

#### 14. Module: Km Balancing & Simulation
- 14.1 Distribution Visualization (Histogram)
- 14.2 Train Ranking by Delta
- 14.3 Outlier Identification
- 14.4 Scenario Creation
- 14.5 Simulation Execution
- 14.6 Scenario Comparison

> **Covers:** Balancing analytics, what-if simulation system, and scenario management.

#### 15. Module: Maintenance Gantt
- 15.1 Timeline View Options
- 15.2 Maintenance Blocks (Color-Coded)
- 15.3 Drag-Drop Scheduling
- 15.4 Constraint Validation
- 15.5 Depot Capacity Heatmap

> **Covers:** Gantt chart implementation, drag-drop interactions, and conflict detection.

---

### Part IV: Technical Architecture

#### 16. System Architecture
- 16.1 High-Level Architecture Diagram
- 16.2 Technology Stack
- 16.3 Monorepo Structure
- 16.4 Service Communication
- 16.5 Key Architectural Decisions

> **Covers:** Overall system design, technology choices with rationale, and project structure.

#### 17. Frontend Architecture
- 17.1 Next.js 14 App Router Structure
- 17.2 Component Organization
- 17.3 State Management (@belt/tools)
- 17.4 API Client Pattern
- 17.5 Blueprint.js Integration
- 17.6 Styling Strategy

> **Covers:** Frontend technical details, component patterns, and state management approach.

#### 18. Backend Architecture
- 18.1 Fastify Server Structure
- 18.2 Route Organization
- 18.3 Service Layer Pattern
- 18.4 Database Access (Drizzle ORM)
- 18.5 Caching Strategy (Redis)
- 18.6 Error Handling

> **Covers:** Backend implementation patterns, API design, and data access layer.

#### 19. Database Design
- 19.1 Entity-Relationship Diagram
- 19.2 Schema Definition (All Tables)
- 19.3 Index Strategy
- 19.4 Data Types & Constraints
- 19.5 Seed Data Requirements

> **Covers:** Complete database schema, relationships, indexes, and realistic seed data specifications.

#### 20. Solver Architecture (Python/OR-Tools)
- 20.1 FastAPI Service Structure
- 20.2 CP-SAT Optimization Model
- 20.3 Objective Function
- 20.4 Constraints Definition
- 20.5 Justification Generation
- 20.6 Fallback Strategy

> **Covers:** Python solver implementation, OR-Tools model, and recommendation engine logic.

#### 21. API Specification
- 21.1 REST Endpoints Inventory
- 21.2 Request/Response Schemas
- 21.3 Error Response Format
- 21.4 Authentication (Demo Scope)

> **Covers:** Complete API documentation with all endpoints, schemas, and examples.

---

### Part V: Implementation Guide

#### 22. Epic & Story Breakdown
- 22.1 Epic Overview
- 22.2 Epic 1: Project Foundation
- 22.3 Epic 2: Dashboard & KPIs
- 22.4 Epic 3: Map & Fleet Visualization
- 22.5 Epic 4: Allocation with Recommendations
- 22.6 Epic 5: Km Balancing & Simulation
- 22.7 Epic 6: Maintenance Gantt
- 22.8 Story Dependencies

> **Covers:** Complete breakdown of work into implementable stories with acceptance criteria.

#### 23. Development Setup
- 23.1 Prerequisites
- 23.2 Environment Variables
- 23.3 Docker Compose Configuration
- 23.4 Database Setup & Seeding
- 23.5 Running the Development Environment

> **Covers:** Step-by-step setup instructions for new developers.

#### 24. Testing Strategy
- 24.1 Unit Testing (Vitest)
- 24.2 E2E Testing (Playwright)
- 24.3 Visual Testing Requirements
- 24.4 Test Coverage Goals

> **Covers:** Testing approach, tools, and mandatory verification requirements.

#### 25. Deployment & Demo Environment
- 25.1 Demo Laptop Setup
- 25.2 Docker Compose Production
- 25.3 Demo Reset Script
- 25.4 Fallback Screens

> **Covers:** How to deploy for demo, reset procedures, and contingency materials.

---

### Part VI: Lessons Learned & Recovery

#### 26. Original Implementation Issues
- 26.1 What Went Wrong
- 26.2 Broken Features Analysis
- 26.3 Technical Debt Accumulated
- 26.4 Root Cause Analysis

> **Covers:** Honest assessment of Claude Code implementation issues to avoid repeating mistakes.

#### 27. Recovery Recommendations
- 27.1 Recommended Approach
- 27.2 Priority Order for Features
- 27.3 Risk Mitigation Strategies
- 27.4 Quality Gates

> **Covers:** Specific recommendations for the human team based on lessons learned.

---

# PART I: BUSINESS CONTEXT & STRATEGY

---

## Chapter 1: Executive Summary

### 1.1 Project Overview

**MMS (Metro Mileage Management System)** is an enterprise solution designed to centralize and automate mileage management for mixed rolling stock fleets exceeding 100 units. The system bridges the gap between Operations and Maintenance departments through predictive, data-driven planning.

**Core Problem Statement:**

Railway operators managing large fleets face three critical pain points:

1. **Over-running trains** - Units exceeding target mileage degrade faster, increasing maintenance costs and reducing asset lifespan
2. **Under-running trains** - Units falling below target mileage represent idle capital with poor return on investment
3. **Manual planning limitations** - Excel-based planning does not scale for 100+ train fleets and cannot optimize across multiple constraints

**Solution Vision:**

MMS addresses all three pain points through:
- Intelligent mileage balancing across the entire fleet
- AI-powered train recommendations with visible justifications
- Predictive maintenance scheduling coordinated with operations
- What-if simulation for strategic planning

### 1.2 Business Objective

**Primary Goal:** Win a competitive bid for a railway fleet management contract through a compelling 15-minute demo presentation.

**Target Audience:**
- Operations Director (decision maker)
- Client's CTO (technical validator)
- Maintenance leadership (domain experts)

**Success Statement:**

> After 15 minutes of demo, the client should think: **"They understand our problem better than the others."**

**Key Differentiators from Competitors:**

| Differentiator | Why It Matters |
|----------------|----------------|
| Km balancing as PRIMARY objective | Competitors treat this as a secondary feature |
| Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations interface | Eliminates departmental silos, reduces rework |
| Visible dead running cost | Hidden cost made explicit and optimizable |
| Accessible what-if simulation | Decision-makers can "play" with scenarios |
| Transparent decision justification | Trust building through explainability, not black box AI |

### 1.3 Success Metrics

**User Success Metrics:**

| User Role | Success Indicator | Measurement |
|-----------|-------------------|-------------|
| Planner | Visualize fleet km distribution in seconds | < 5s to see complete distribution |
| Planner | Receive justified train recommendation | 3+ reasons per recommendation |
| Planner | Simulate scenarios before deciding | Side-by-side comparison in < 10s |
| Dispatcher | Allocate train to service quickly | < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks |
| Dispatcher | See current train positions on map | Real-time or < 30s refresh |
| Manager | Executive dashboard view | All KPIs visible without navigation |
| All Users | Trust the system recommendations | Decision justification always visible |

**Business Success Metrics:**

| Metric | Target |
|--------|--------|
| Demo Duration | 15 minutes maximum |
| Demo Stability | 3 consecutive rehearsals without failures |
| Domain Terminology | 100% correct EN 15380 railway terms |
| Client Reaction | "They understand our problem better than the others" |

**Technical Success Metrics:**

| Requirement | Target | Rationale |
|-------------|--------|-----------|
| Allocation Time | < 30 seconds for 100 trains | Dispatcher won't wait longer |
| Screen Loading | < 3 seconds | Credibility in live demo |
| Map Update | < 5 seconds | "Real-time" perception |
| Simulation Response | < 10 seconds | Maintain interactive feel |
| Demo Stability | No crashes for 15 minutes | Critical for presentation |

### 1.4 Timeline & Constraints

**Project Timeline:** 12 weeks

**Milestone Schedule:**

| Week | Milestone | Deliverable |
|------|-----------|-------------|
| Week 4 | Core solver working | Proof of technical capability |
| Week 6 | Navigable UI with mocked data | Visual credibility established |
| Week 8 | End-to-end flow working | Integration proof complete |

| Week 12 | Demo-ready with fallbacks | Risk mitigation complete |

**Technical Constraints:**

| Demo-only scope | No production hardening | Use seed data, skip authentication |

| OR-Tools learning curve | Development complexity | Simplify model, use greedy fallback |

**Key Assumptions:**

1. Demo environment runs on stable network (no offline mode needed)
2. Seed data is pre-generated (no real EAM integration)
3. Single-tenant, no multi-organization support required

## Chapter 2: Railway Domain Fundamentals

### 2.1 Essential Glossary

All UI labels and system messages MUST use standard EN 15380 railway terminology. Using incorrect terms destroys credibility with railway professionals.

**Core Terms:**

| Term | Definition | NOT Acceptable |
|------|------------|----------------|
| Rolling Stock | Railway vehicles requiring maintenance (trains, carriages, locomotives) | Vehicle, Asset, Unit |
| Fleet | Group of rolling stock sharing the same manufacturer/model and maintenance profile | Group, Class, Collection |
| Depot | Designated maintenance facility for a fleet | Garage, Workshop, Station |
| Train Fitness | Binary operational readiness status (fit/unfit) | Availability, Health, Status |
| Run Assignment | Daily allocation of train IDs to routes/services | Dispatch, Schedule, Trip |
| Dead Running | Non-revenue movement of train between locations | Empty movement, Repositioning |
| Service | A scheduled revenue-generating journey from origin to destination | Route, Trip, Run |
| Mileage Balancing | Strategy to equalize wear across fleet by controlling km distribution | Usage balancing, Wear distribution |

**Maintenance Terms:**

| Term | Definition | Typical Interval |
|------|------------|------------------|
| PM1 | Preventive Maintenance Level 1 (light inspection) | Every 15,000 km |
| PM2 | Preventive Maintenance Level 2 (medium service) | Every 45,000 km |
| HM | Heavy Maintenance (major overhaul) | Every 90,000 km |
| OVH | Overhaul (complete refurbishment) | Every 6 years or 500,000 km |

**Operational Terms:**

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| TCO | Total Cost of Ownership over fleet lifecycle |
| Overrun | Train that has exceeded its target mileage |
| Underrun | Train that falls short of its target mileage |
| Variance | Statistical measure of mileage distribution spread across fleet |
| Aptitude | Train's certification for specific line/speed requirements |

### 2.2 Business Entities & Relationships

**Entity Relationship Model:**

```
┌─────────────┐         ┌─────────────┐
│    Fleet    │────────│    Train    │
│             │   1:N  │             │
│ - name      │        │ - train_id  │
│ - pm1_km    │        │ - current_km│
│ - pm2_km    │        │ - target_km │
│ - hm_km     │        │ - fitness   │
└─────────────┘        └──────┬──────┘
                              │
       ┌──────────────────────┼──────────────────────┐
       │                      │                      │
       ▼                      ▼                      ▼
┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐        ┌─────────────┐
│    Depot    │        │ Allocation  │        │ Maintenance │
│             │        │             │        │  Schedule   │
│ - name      │        │ - date      │        │             │
│ - capacity  │        │ - status    │        │ - type      │
│ - manhours  │        │ - score     │        │ - date      │
└─────────────┘        │ - reasons   │        │ - depot     │
                       └──────┬──────┘        └─────────────┘
                              │
                              ▼
                       ┌─────────────┐
                       │   Service   │
                       │             │
                       │ - code      │
                       │ - origin    │
                       │ - dest      │
                       │ - distance  │
                       └─────────────┘
```

**Cardinality Rules:**

- One Fleet has many Trains (1:N)
- One Train belongs to one Fleet (N:1)
- One Depot can host trains from multiple Fleets (N:M)
- One Train has one Base Depot (N:1)
- One Service can have many Allocations (different dates) (1:N)
- One Train can have many Allocations (different services) (1:N)
- One Train can have many Maintenance Schedules (1:N)

### 2.3 Train Lifecycle & Maintenance Hierarchy

**Maintenance Cascade:**

```
Fleet Type (e.g., Metro Series 3400)
 └─ Maintenance Profile
     ├─ PM1 (every 15,000 km)
     │   └─ Duration: 1 day
     │   └─ Tasks: Inspection, fluid top-up, brake check
     │
     ├─ PM2 (every 45,000 km)
     │   └─ Duration: 3 days
     │   └─ Tasks: Component replacement, deep inspection
     │   └─ Note: Includes PM1 tasks
     │
     ├─ HM (every 90,000 km)
     │   └─ Duration: 7-14 days
     │   └─ Tasks: Major component overhaul, bogie work
     │   └─ Note: Includes PM1 + PM2 tasks
     │
     └─ OVH (every 6 years or 500,000 km)
         └─ Duration: 30-60 days
         └─ Tasks: Complete refurbishment
         └─ Note: Resets maintenance counters
```

**Train State Machine:**

```
    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
    │                                             │
    ▼                                             │
┌───────┐     ┌────────────┐     ┌────────────┐  │
│  FIT  │────▶│ IN-SERVICE │────▶│ AVAILABLE  │──┘
│       │     │            │     │            │
└───┬───┘     └────────────┘     └─────┬──────┘
    │                                  │
    │         ┌────────────┐           │
    └────────▶│   UNFIT    │◀──────────┘
              │            │
              └─────┬──────┘
                    │
                    ▼
              ┌────────────┐
              │MAINTENANCE │
              │            │
              └─────┬──────┘
                    │
                    ▼
              ┌────────────┐
              │    FIT     │
              └────────────┘
```

### 2.4 Critical Domain Rules (Unknown Unknowns)

These are domain constraints that seasoned railway professionals know intuitively but are rarely documented. Violating these rules destroys credibility instantly.

**Hard Constraints (Never Violate):**

1. **Unfit trains are NEVER assigned to service** - No exception, no override
2. **Maintenance windows are sacred** - A train scheduled for PM cannot be reassigned
3. **Aptitude requirements are binary** - A train either can or cannot run on a line
4. **Depot capacity is physical** - You cannot exceed track slots, period
5. **Service cannot be cancelled to balance km** - Operations > Optimization

**Soft Constraints (Optimize Within):**

1. **Km balancing priority** - Under-running trains should get high-km services
2. **Dead running minimization** - Prefer trains that end near next service start
3. **Maintenance depot proximity** - Schedule services that end near maintenance depot
4. **Crew familiarity** - Same crews prefer same train types (not modeled in MVP)

**Critical Timing Rules:**

| Rule | Specification |
|------|---------------|
| PM1 tolerance window | Can be ±500 km from trigger |
| PM2 tolerance window | Can be ±1,000 km from trigger |
| HM tolerance window | Can be ±2,000 km from trigger |
| Overrun alert | Train at 95% of next PM triggers warning |
| Critical overrun | Train at 105% of next PM triggers alert |

### 2.5 ECM Certification Framework (EU Regulation 2019/779)

MMS aligns with the Entity in Charge of Maintenance (ECM) certification requirements as defined by EU Regulation 2019/779. This framework establishes the legal responsibilities for railway vehicle maintenance across the European railway network.

**ECM Function Mapping:**

| Function | Responsibility | MMS Feature |
|----------|----------------|-------------|
| **ECM-F1** | Maintenance Management | Dashboard KPIs, compliance tracking, audit trail |
| **ECM-F2** | Maintenance Development | Scheduler optimization, plan generation, interval definitions |
| **ECM-F3** | Fleet Maintenance Management | Real-time fleet status, conflict detection, availability monitoring |
| **ECM-F4** | Maintenance Delivery | Work order generation, depot allocation, resource planning |

**Compliance Mapping:**

| Requirement | ECM Article | MMS Implementation |
|-------------|-------------|-------------------|
| Risk assessment | Art. 5.2 | Conflict detection, overdue warnings, overrun alerts |
| Competence management | Art. 5.3 | Future: resource skill tracking (Phase 2+) |
| Documentation | Art. 5.4 | Audit trail, maintenance history, decision logging |
| Performance monitoring | Art. 5.5 | KPI dashboard, fleet availability metrics, variance tracking |

**Critical Note:** The ECM framework requires that the entity responsible for maintenance (operator or subcontractor) maintains full traceability of all maintenance decisions. MMS provides this through:
- Immutable audit logs of all scheduling decisions
- Justification records for allocation recommendations
- Historical mileage tracking with timestamps

### 2.6 RAMS Compliance (EN 50126-1)

MMS supports RAMS (Reliability, Availability, Maintainability, Safety) principles as defined in EN 50126-1, the European standard for railway dependability.

**RAMS Integration:**

| Aspect | Standard | MMS Feature | Target |
|--------|----------|-------------|--------|
| **Reliability** | EN 50126-1 §6 | MTBF tracking, failure prediction | TBD by operator |
| **Availability** | EN 50126-1 §7 | Fleet availability KPIs, downtime monitoring | 95-99% |
| **Maintainability** | EN 50126-1 §8 | MTTR optimization, workload balancing | <24h per PM1 |
| **Safety** | EN 50126-1 §9 | Critical task prioritization, overrun prevention | Zero safety overruns |

**Key Performance Indicators:**

| KPI | Formula | Target | Critical Threshold |
|-----|---------|--------|-------------------|
| Fleet Availability | (Fit Trains / Total Trains) × 100 | ≥97% | <95% triggers alert |
| MTBF (Mean Time Between Failures) | Total Operating Hours / Number of Failures | TBD | Operator-defined |
| MTTR (Mean Time To Repair) | Total Downtime / Number of Repairs | ≤24h | >48h triggers escalation |
| Schedule Adherence | (On-Time Maintenance / Scheduled Maintenance) × 100 | ≥95% | <90% requires review |

### 2.7 Terminology Equivalence

MMS uses aviation-derived terminology (A/B/C/D Checks) internally for developer familiarity, but provides full equivalence mapping to railway standards for operator communication.

**Maintenance Level Equivalence:**

| MMS Term | Railway Standard (EN 15380) | Aviation Origin | Typical Interval | Duration |
|----------|----------------------------|-----------------|--------------------|----------|
| A-Check | PM1 (Daily/Service) | Line maintenance | Daily / 15,000 km | 15-60 min |
| B-Check | PM2 (Intermediate) | A-Check | 45,000-50,000 km | 4-16 hours |
| C-Check | PM3 (Scheduled) | C-Check | 90,000-100,000 km | 1-3 days |
| D-Overhaul | HM (Heavy Maintenance) | D-Check | 500,000-800,000 km | 2-6 weeks |

> **Note:** MMS uses aviation-derived terminology (A/B/C/D) internally for developer familiarity. All user-facing displays and reports use EN 15380 railway terminology (PM1/PM2/PM3/HM) to maintain credibility with railway professionals.

**UIC IRS Reference:**

The International Union of Railways (UIC) publishes International Railway Solutions (IRS) that complement EN standards:

| Document | Title | Relevance to MMS |
|----------|-------|------------------|
| IRS 30100-3 | Maintenance Terms | Terminology standardization |
| IRS 30200 | RAM Demonstrable values | KPI calculation methods |
| IRS 70711 | Rolling Stock Maintenance | Maintenance interval guidelines |

### 2.8 What the System Does NOT Do

Explicit scope boundaries to prevent scope creep and set correct expectations:

**Phase 2+ (Not in Demo):**

| Feature | Reason for Exclusion | Target Phase |
|---------|---------------------|--------------|
| MILP long-term optimization | Exceeds complexity budget | Phase 2 |
| Real EAM system integration | Requires client environment | Phase 2 |
| Disruption management | Different architecture needed | Phase 3 |
| Integrated crew scheduling | Doubles complexity | Phase 3 |
| Native mobile application | Responsive web sufficient | If demanded |
| Multi-language support | English sufficient for demo | Production |
| Real-time GPS tracking | Simulated positions sufficient | Production |
| Predictive maintenance ML | Beyond demo scope | Phase 3 |

**Explicitly NOT Goals:**

- This is NOT a full EAM (Enterprise Asset Management) system
- This is NOT a crew scheduling system
- This is NOT a timetabling system
- This is NOT a revenue management system
- This does NOT handle disruption/delay management
- This does NOT integrate with signaling systems

## Chapter 3: Market Analysis & Competitive Positioning

### 3.1 Competitor Landscape

**Primary Competitors:**

| Competitor | Focus Area | Strength | Weakness |
|------------|------------|----------|----------|
| DELMIA Quintiq | Large-scale optimization | Powerful solver, enterprise scale | Complex, expensive, long implementation |
| Tracsis | UK rail operations | Strong timetabling, UK market presence | Less focus on maintenance integration |
| Railnova | Rail fleet telematics | Real-time data, IoT integration | Less sophisticated optimization |
| SignatureRail | Fleet maintenance | Clean UI, workflow focus | Limited optimization, basic balancing |

### 3.2 SignatureRail Deep Analysis

SignatureRail represents the most direct competitor in terms of user experience and feature set.

**SignatureRail Strengths:**

- Clean, modern enterprise UI
- Well-organized workflow panels
- Good use of data tables
- Professional color scheme
- Clear navigation structure

**SignatureRail Weaknesses (MMS Opportunities):**

1. **Basic km tracking without optimization** - Shows variance but doesn't recommend actions
2. **No visible justifications** - Recommendations are black box
3. **Limited simulation capability** - Cannot run what-if scenarios
4. **Separate maintenance and operations** - Silos remain
5. **No map visualization** - Spatial context missing

### 3.3 Market Gaps & Opportunities

**Gap Analysis:**

| Gap | Market Need | MMS Solution |
|-----|-------------|--------------|
| Passive vs Active Balancing | Systems show variance but don't optimize | AI-powered recommendations with justifications |
| Black Box Decisions | Users don't understand why system recommends | Transparent 3+ reason justifications |
| Operations/Maintenance Silos | Separate systems, manual coordination | Unified interface for both departments |
| Strategic Planning | Day-to-day focus, no simulation | What-if scenarios for planning |
| Dead Running Visibility | Hidden cost, rarely tracked | Explicit display and optimization |

### 3.4 MMS Differentiators

**Primary Differentiators:**

1. **Km Balancing as Core Objective**
   - Not a secondary feature or afterthought
   - Drives all allocation recommendations
   - Visible in dashboard KPIs

2. **Transparent Justifications**
   - Every recommendation shows 3+ reasons
   - Human-readable explanations
   - Builds trust vs black box

3. **Unified Maintenance ↔ Operations**
   - Single interface for both departments
   - Recommendations consider maintenance schedule
   - Reduces coordination overhead

4. **Accessible Simulation**
   - Decision-makers can test scenarios
   - Side-by-side comparison
   - No technical expertise required

5. **Visible Dead Running**
   - Hidden cost made explicit
   - Factored into optimization
   - ROI clearly demonstrable

## Chapter 4: Demo Strategy & Presentation

### 4.1 Core Principle: Depth Over Breadth

> **Mantra:** "Solving 1 pain point extremely well > solving 10 pain points poorly"

**Strategic Focus:**

The demo should demonstrate deep understanding of the km balancing problem rather than showcase many features superficially. Every feature shown must:

1. Be 100% functional (no "coming soon")
2. Use correct railway terminology
3. Show real (simulated but realistic) data
4. Complete end-to-end without errors

### 4.2 Wow Moments

These are specific moments in the demo designed to create impact:

**Wow Moment 1: The Variance Discovery**
- Dashboard loads showing 18% variance (yellow warning)
- Client recognizes their exact problem
- "They're showing us what we live every day"

**Wow Moment 2: The Justification Card**
- Recommendation shows not just WHAT but WHY
- 3+ human-readable reasons
- "Finally, a system that explains itself"

**Wow Moment 3: The What-If**
- Create scenario, adjust allocations
- See projected impact in < 10 seconds
- "I can test my ideas before committing"

**Wow Moment 4: The Map Context**
- See where all trains are spatially
- Click train, see km delta immediately
- "This gives us operational awareness"

### 4.3 Credibility Checklist

Before demo, verify:

- [ ] All terminology is EN 15380 compliant
- [ ] Depot names are real Portuguese locations
- [ ] Service codes follow realistic patterns (IC, R, U)
- [ ] Maintenance intervals are realistic (PM1: 15k km)
- [ ] Train IDs follow realistic naming (UTE-3400-xxx)
- [ ] Km values are in plausible ranges (30k-60k)
- [ ] Variance percentages are realistic (10-25%)
- [ ] Map shows Portugal correctly positioned

### 4.4 Anti-Patterns to Avoid

**NEVER do these in demo:**

| Anti-Pattern | Why It Fails | Alternative |
|--------------|--------------|-------------|
| Show stub screens | Destroys credibility | Show fewer, complete features |
| Use placeholder data | "Lorem ipsum" is unprofessional | Use realistic railway data |
| Demo features that don't work | Client will try to break it | Only show stable features |
| Use generic software terms | "Vehicle" instead of "Train" | Use railway terminology |
| Show loading spinners | Suggests slow system | Pre-cache or mock fast responses |
| Apologize for missing features | Shows lack of confidence | Present what exists as intentional |

### 4.5 Presentation Script (15 minutes)

**Minutes 0-2: Context Setting**
- "We understand your km balancing challenge"
- Show dashboard with variance indicator
- "This is what you see every day"

**Minutes 2-5: The Problem Quantified**
- Click variance to see distribution
- Show outliers (5 over-running, 4 under-running)
- "These 9 trains are costing you money"

**Minutes 5-8: The Solution in Action**
- Navigate to Allocation
- Select a pending service
- Show recommendations with justifications
- "Not just WHAT to assign, but WHY"
- Confirm allocation with one click

**Minutes 8-11: Planning Ahead**
- Navigate to Simulation
- Create what-if scenario
- Adjust allocations for under-running trains
- Show projected variance improvement
- "Test your strategy before committing"

**Minutes 11-13: The Big Picture**
- Return to Dashboard
- Show map with train positions
- Show depot capacity
- "Everything in one view"

**Minutes 13-15: Q&A Buffer**
- Prepared questions if no questions asked
- Have fallback screens ready

### 4.6 FAQ & Fallback Plans

**Anticipated Questions:**

| Question | Prepared Answer |
|----------|-----------------|
| "How does the solver work?" | "CP-SAT optimization balancing multiple constraints - we can show the logic" |
| "Can it integrate with our EAM?" | "Yes, REST API design allows integration with any EAM system" |
| "What about real-time GPS?" | "Architecture supports real-time feeds, demo uses simulated positions" |
| "How long to implement?" | "Core system in 3 months, customization depends on requirements" |
| "What if solver times out?" | "Fallback to simpler ranking ensures always having recommendations" |

**Fallback Plans:**

| Failure | Fallback |
|---------|----------|
| Solver timeout | Pre-computed recommendations in database |
| Database connection lost | Static JSON fallback data |
| Map fails to load | Table view of train positions |
| Browser crash | Second laptop with same setup |
| Network issues | Everything runs locally |

  1048→
  1049→**Frustrations:**
  1050→
  1051→1. "Data is scattered across 5 different systems"
  1052→2. "I need my analysts to build every report"
  1053→3. "Can't drill down from summary to details"
  1054→4. "Numbers don't match between systems"
  1055→5. "Takes days to answer simple board questions"
  1056→
  1057→**Success Moment:**
  1058→> "I walked into the board meeting and had all the answers at my fingertips."
  1059→
  1060→**Critical Need:** Dashboard with executive view, no navigation required
  1061→
  1062→### 5.4 Design Implications per Persona
  1063→
  1064→**Paul (Planner) Design Requirements:**
  1065→
  1066→| Need | Design Solution |
  1067→|------|-----------------|
  1068→| Long sessions (4-8h) | Dark mode to reduce eye strain |
  1069→| Complex data | Dense information display, data tables |
  1070→| Frequent actions | Keyboard shortcuts for power users |
  1071→| Pattern recognition | Visual indicators, color coding |
  1072→| Planning horizon | Timeline views (Gantt), calendar integration |
  1073→| Depot context | Capacity heatmaps, utilization charts |
  1074→
  1075→**Diana (Dispatcher) Design Requirements:**
  1076→
  1077→| Need | Design Solution |
  1078→|------|-----------------|
  1079→| Speed critical | Instant responses (< 3s) |
  1080→| Quick decisions | Single-click actions, confirmation shortcuts |
  1081→| Spatial awareness | Map always visible |
  1082→| Trust in recommendations | Visible justifications |
  1083→| Shift handover | Status at a glance, no hidden states |
  1084→| Error recovery | Undo actions, clear error messages |
  1085→
  1086→**Gabriel (Manager) Design Requirements:**
  1087→
  1088→| Need | Design Solution |
  1089→|------|-----------------|
  1090→| Limited time | KPIs front and center |
  1091→| Big picture | Aggregated metrics, trends |
  1092→| Touch friendly | Large tap targets for tablet |
  1093→| Presentation ready | Clean visuals, exportable |
  1094→| Drill down | Click any number for details |
  1095→| Mobile alerts | Responsive design, notification support |
  1096→

  1099→## Chapter 6: User Journeys
  1100→
  1101→### 6.1 Journey 1: Allocating a Train to Service (Diana)
  1102→
  1103→**Context:**
  1104→Diana arrives at the operations center for her morning shift. The timetable shows that service IC 501 (Lisbon to Porto, 336 km) needs a train assigned for tomorrow's departure.
  1105→
  1106→**Scene 1: The Request**
  1107→
  1108→Diana opens MMS and navigates to the Allocation screen. She selects tomorrow's date using the date picker.
  1109→
  1110→*Screen State:*
  1111→- Left panel: List of pending services for selected date
  1112→- IC 501 highlighted: "Lisbon → Porto | 336 km | Dep: 08:15"
  1113→- Status: Unassigned (red indicator)
  1114→
  1115→**Scene 2: Service Selection**
  1116→
  1117→Diana clicks on IC 501. The service details panel expands showing:
  1118→- Route: Lisbon Santa Apolónia → Porto Campanhã
  1119→- Distance: 336 km
  1120→- Departure: 08:15
  1121→- Arrival: 11:45
  1122→- Required aptitude: North Line (220 km/h capable)
  1123→
  1124→*Action:* Diana clicks "Get Recommendations"
  1125→
  1126→**Scene 3: The Recommendation**
  1127→
  1128→Within 3 seconds, MMS presents a ranked list of 5 suitable trains.
  1129→
  1130→*Screen State:*
  1131→```
  1132→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  1133→│ Recommendations for IC 501                                   │
  1134→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1135→│ 1. UTE-3400-012                          Score: 94/100      │
  1136→│    ├── ✓ Km 12% below fleet average (balancing priority)    │
  1137→│    ├── ✓ Service ends 5km from Porto depot                  │
  1138→│    ├── ✓ PM1 scheduled in 3 days at Porto                   │
  1139→│    └── ✓ North Line aptitude confirmed                      │
  1140→│    [Assign]                                                  │
  1141→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1142→│ 2. UTE-3400-008                          Score: 87/100      │
  1143→│    ├── ✓ Km 8% below fleet average                          │
  1144→│    ├── ✗ Dead running 25km from current location            │
  1145→│    └── ✓ North Line aptitude confirmed                      │
  1146→│    [Assign]                                                  │
  1147→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  1148→```
  1149→
  1150→**Scene 4: The Decision**
  1151→
  1152→Diana reviews the justifications. The first recommendation explains exactly WHY this train is best:
  1153→- It's under-running (helps balance fleet)
  1154→- It ends near Porto depot (efficient for next service or maintenance)
  1155→- PM1 is coming up, and Porto has a slot (maintenance coordination)
  1156→
  1157→Diana clicks "Assign" on UTE-3400-012.
  1158→
  1159→**Scene 5: Confirmation**

  1161→*Feedback:*

  1166→**Journey Metrics:**
  1167→- Total time: < 2 minutes
  1168→- Total clicks: 4 (Date → Service → Recommend → Assign)
  1169→- Decisions made: 1 (which train)
  1170→- Information provided: 4 justifications
  1171→
  1172→**Requirements Revealed:**
  1173→- FR-3.1: List pending services
  1174→- FR-3.2: Generate train recommendations
  1175→- FR-3.3: Display justifications
  1176→- FR-3.4: Confirm allocation
  1177→- NFR-1.1: < 30 seconds for recommendations
  1178→- NFR-4.1: < 2 minutes, < 5 clicks

  1180→### 6.2 Journey 2: Identifying and Resolving Imbalance (Paul)
  1181→
  1182→**Context:**
  1183→Paul starts his Monday morning by checking fleet health. He's been worried about the mileage variance creeping up over the past few weeks.
  1184→
  1185→**Scene 1: The Discovery**
  1186→
  1187→Paul opens MMS Dashboard. The Km Variance KPI card immediately shows:
  1188→- Value: 18%
  1189→- Status: Yellow warning icon
  1190→- Trend: ↑ up from 15% last week
  1191→
  1192→*Action:* Paul clicks on the variance KPI card.
  1193→
  1194→**Scene 2: The Analysis**
  1195→
  1196→The system navigates to the Balancing Analysis screen, showing:
  1197→
  1198→*Screen State:*
  1199→```
  1200→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  1201→│ Fleet Km Distribution                    Variance: 18%  ⚠️  │
  1202→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1203→│                                                             │
  1204→│     Histogram showing km distribution                       │
  1205→│                                                             │
  1206→│  Trains │    ▓▓                                             │
  1207→│         │    ▓▓  ▓▓                                         │
  1208→│         │    ▓▓  ▓▓  ▓▓▓▓                                   │
  1209→│         │ ▓▓ ▓▓  ▓▓  ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓                               │
  1210→│         │ ▓▓ ▓▓  ▓▓  ▓▓▓▓  ▓▓  ▓▓  ▓▓                       │
  1211→│         └────────────│────────────────── Km                 │
  1212→│              Under   │ Average   Over                       │
  1213→│                   45,000 km                                 │
  1214→│                                                             │
  1215→│  Target Range: ±10% (40,500 - 49,500 km)                   │
  1216→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  1217→
  1218→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  1219→│ Outliers (>15% from average)                                │
  1220→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1221→│ OVER-RUNNING (reduce allocations)                           │
  1222→│ • UTE-3400-025  +23%  52,200 km  ⚠️ PM1 overdue             │
  1223→│ • UTE-2200-018  +19%  50,100 km                             │
  1224→│ • UTE-3400-031  +18%  49,800 km                             │
  1225→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1226→│ UNDER-RUNNING (prioritize for services)                     │
  1227→│ • UTE-2200-015  -25%  33,750 km                             │
  1228→│ • UTE-3400-003  -21%  35,550 km                             │
  1229→│ • UTE-2200-009  -18%  36,900 km                             │
  1230→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  1231→```
  1232→
  1233→Paul can clearly see 5 over-running trains and 4 significantly under-running trains.
  1234→
  1235→**Scene 3: The Simulation**
  1236→
  1237→Paul wants to test a rebalancing strategy before committing.
  1238→
  1239→*Action:* Paul clicks "New Scenario"
  1240→
  1241→A dialog appears:
  1242→- Scenario name: "Week 2 Rebalance"
  1243→- Base date: 2026-01-05
  1244→
  1245→Paul clicks "Create Scenario" and enters scenario mode (indicated by orange border around the screen).
  1246→
  1247→**Scene 4: Making Changes**
  1248→
  1249→In scenario mode, Paul:
  1250→1. Selects the 4 under-running trains
  1251→2. Views upcoming high-km services for next 30 days
  1252→3. Reassigns these services to prioritize under-running trains
  1253→
  1254→*Action:* Paul clicks "Run Simulation"
  1255→
  1256→**Scene 5: Projected Results**
  1257→
  1258→Within 10 seconds, the simulation completes:
  1259→
  1260→```
  1261→┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
  1262→│ Simulation Results - "Week 2 Rebalance"                     │
  1263→├─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┤
  1264→│                         CURRENT    PROJECTED    CHANGE      │
  1265→│ Variance                  18%         14%        -4% ✓     │
  1266→│ Dead Running (30d)      2,450 km    2,580 km    +5% ⚠️     │
  1267→│ Over-running trains         5           3        -2 ✓      │
  1268→│ Under-running trains        4           2        -2 ✓      │
  1269→└─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘
  1270→```
  1271→
  1272→**Scene 6: The Decision**
  1273→
  1274→Paul sees the trade-off: variance improves 4%, but dead running increases slightly. He decides the trade-off is acceptable.
  1275→
  1276→*Action:* Paul clicks "Apply to Live Plan"
  1277→
  1278→Confirmation dialog: "This will update 12 allocations. Continue?"
  1279→
  1280→Paul confirms. Changes are applied.
  1281→
  1282→**Journey Metrics:**
  1283→- Total time: < 10 minutes
  1284→- Key insight: Quantified variance problem
  1285→- Action taken: Rebalancing plan applied
  1286→- Confidence: High (saw projected impact before committing)
  1287→
  1288→**Requirements Revealed:**
  1289→- FR-4.1: Visualize km distribution
  1290→- FR-4.2: Rank trains by delta
  1291→- FR-4.3: Show fleet average
  1292→- FR-4.4: Identify outliers
  1293→- FR-5.1: Create what-if scenario
  1294→- FR-5.2: Modify future allocations
  1295→- FR-5.3: Run simulation
  1296→- FR-5.5: Apply scenario
